content.full_text:
City of Petaluma 11 English Street
Post Office Box 61 · Petaluma, California 94953

OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY (707) 778-4362
Mayor January 8, 1992
M. Patricia Hilligoss

Councilmembers
Jack W. Cavanagh
Michael Davis
Bonnie Nelson
Nancy C. Read
Brian Sobel
Lynn C. Woolsey

California Fair Political Practices
Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

ATTN: Scott Hallabrin

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

This letter is intended to serve as a formal request for formal written advice from the
FPPC regarding conflict of interest issues. If any further facts or information are necessary or
required for such formal written advice, please contact me.

FACTS

For approximately 13 years, Envirotech Operating Systems, Inc. ("EOS") has operated
the City sewer plant under a service contract. A copy of said service agreement with
amendments is attached as Attachment A. When it became apparent to the City in 1988 that a
new plant was needed due to the deterioration of the old plant and the growth within the City,
the city, through City Manager John Scharer, entered into discussions and negotiations with
EOS to determine the feasibility of entering into a service agreement whereby EOS would
design, build, own and operate a private treatment plant for the City under the Privatization
Act (Government Code Section 54250 et seq.). In July of 1989, as a result of those
discussions and negotiations, a proposed Memorandum of Understanding ("MOU") was
brought to the City Council for discussion and review. Mayor Patricia Hilligoss,
Councilmembers Brian Sobel, Jack Cavanagh, Michael Davis, and Lynn Woolsey and City
Manager John Scharer participated in the discussions. The essence of the MOU was that the
City would exclusively negotiate with EOS for 18 months to arrive at a service agreement
regarding the EOS proposal to design, build, own and operate the plant. During that time,
EOS would seek an exemption from the Public Utilities Commission. After direction from the
Council, negotiations were conducted by Mr. Scharer and other staff wherein the MOU
was revised. The revised MOU was brought back to the Council in April of 1991 at which
time the City Council authorized the City Manager to enter into the MOU. Mayor Hilligoss
and Councilmembers Sobel, Cavanagh, Davis and Woolsey voted in favor of the proposed
MOU. Mr. Scharer participated in the staff presentation and discussion. Subsequently, EOS
assigned its rights to an entity solely owned by EOS entitled "EOS-Petaluma". A copy of said
MOU is attached as Attachment B. Preliminary estimates of the cost of construction of such a

Scott Hallibrin
January 8, 1992
Page 2

plant by EOS-Petaluma is $15-20 million dollars. It is unknown how the construction and
operation of the potential plant would affect the profits or other financial aspects of the various
companies, nor is it known how the plant would be financed by EOS-Petaluma at this time.

John Scharer has been the City Manager for Petaluma since 1981. Ms. Hilligoss was
Mayor in 1988 and has continued as a Mayor of the Council up to the present date.
Councilmembers Sobel, Cavanagh, Davis and Woolsey were Councilmembers in 1988 and
have continued on the Council to the present date.

According to 1990-1991 Form 721 prepared by Mr. Scharer and filed with the City
Clerk on February 22, 1991, he owned at that time 130 shares of stock with a value of
approximately $4,800 in Waste Management, Inc. It is my understanding that the stock was
purchased in November of 1989. According to the 1990-1991 Form 721 prepared by Mayor
Hilligoss and submitted to the City Clerk on March 26, 1991, she purchased, in January of
1990, 10 shares of stock, with a value of approximately $3,700 in Waste Management, Inc.

According to City of Petaluma Councilmember Michael Davis (as indicated in his
contribution of $500 from Waste Management. Empire Waste Management has been a wholly
owned subsidiary of Waste Management of North America which is a subsidiary of Waste
Management, Inc.

According to City of Petaluma Councilmember Jack Cavanagh (as indicated in his
Form 490 executed on January 30, 1989), he received on November 3, 1988 a campaign
contribution of $500 from Empire Waste Management of North America.

According to City of Petaluma Councilmember Brian Sobel (as indicated in his Form
490 executed on January 31, 1991), he received on October 31, 1990 a campaign contribution
of $200 from Waste Management of North America.

In July of 1990, Waste Management, Inc. owned 23 percent of Wheelabrator who was
the sole owner of EOS. In April of 1991, it is my understanding that Waste Management, Inc.
was a majority owner of Wheelabrator, Inc. which in turn owned EOS. Subsequent to the
April, 1991, meeting, EOS then assigned its rights under the MOU to an entity owned by EOS
entitled EOS-Petaluma.

Waste Management, Inc. presently has outstanding approximately 493 million shares
and is listed on the New York Stock Exchange. Therefore, the stock owned by Mr. Scharer
and Ms. Hilligoss were approximately .000000263 and .000000202 percent, respectively, of
the outstanding shares of Waste Management, Inc., a company twice removed from EOS and
three times removed from EOS-Petaluma. It is my understanding that at the time the Mayor
and City Manager purchased the stock in Waste Management, Inc., Wheelabrator
Technologies, Inc. was not a subsidiary of Waste Management, Inc. and only became a
subsidiary approximately six months prior to the April, 1991, decision. Neither Mr. Scharer
or Mayor Hilligoss was aware of the connection between Waste Management, Inc. and EOS at
the time of the April, 1991 City Council meeting and vote.

It is my understanding that stock dividend received by Mr. Scharer and Ms. Hilligoss
were approximately $57 per year and $44 a year, respectively. Subsequent to the April, 1991,

Scott Hallibrin
January 8, 1992
Page 3

vote, both Hilligoss and Scharer sold their stock on the open market for approximately
$4,600.00 and $3,500.00, respectively.

After the MOU was executed, EOS-Petaluma applied for exemption from the Public
Utilities Commission. After public hearings, the Commission denied EOS-Petaluma's
exemption without prejudice. EOS-Petaluma's application was the first application under the
Privatization Act which even according to the administrative law judge was very difficult to
understand and interpret. In any event, the Public Utilities Commission denied the exemption
without prejudice.

It is anticipated that further negotiations will be conducted between the City (through
City Manager John Scharer) and EOS-Petaluma to revise the MOU and that the revised MOU
would be brought to the Council for action. If the new MOU is approved and EOS-Petaluma
obtains an exemption from the Public Utilities Commission, it is anticipated that the City,
again through City Manager John Scharer, will negotiate a service agreement with EOS-
Petaluma as well as conduct hearings on environmental and land use issues, all of which are
subject to discussion and approval by the Council.

It is also anticipated that there will be a number of public work shops and study
sessions to discuss various issues regarding design of plant and other general issues regarding
sewage treatment.

The questions posed to the FPPC are as follows:

1. May City Manager John Scharer participate in negotiations with respect to a
new MOU?

2. May Mayor Patricia Hilligoss participate in discussions and vote on the revised
MOU?

3. May Councilmembers Jack Cavanagh, Brian Sobel, and Michael Davis
participate in discussions and vote on the revised MOU?

4. If a revised MOU is approved and EOS-Petaluma obtains an exemption from
the Public Utilities Commission, may the City Manager participate in
negotiations with respect to the intended service agreement?

5. If a revised MOU is approved and EOS-Petaluma obtains an exemption from
the Public Utilities Commission, may Mayor Hilligoss participate in discussions
and vote on the proposed service agreement?

6. If a revised MOU is approved and EOS-Petaluma obtains an exemption from
the Public Utilities Commission, may Councilmembers Jack Cavanagh, Brian
Sobel, and Michael Davis participate in discussions and vote on the proposed
service agreement?

7. May the City Manager participate in discussions and staff presentations to the
City Council pertaining to land use entitlement and environmental issues
pertaining to the construction and/or operation of the treatment plant?

Scott Hallibrin
January 8, 1992
Page 4

8.      May Mayor Hilligoss participate and vote with respect to land use entitlements
        and environmental issues pertaining to the construction and/or operation of the
        sewage plant?

9.      May Councilmembers Jack Cavanagh, Brian Sobel, and Michael Davis
        participate and vote with respect to land use entitlements and environmental
        issues pertaining to the construction and/or operation of the sewage plant?

10.     May City Manager John Scharer participate in contemplated public workshops
        and study sessions regarding design of plant and other general issues of sewage
        treatment?

11.     May Mayor Hilligoss participate in contemplated public workshops and study
        sessions regarding design of plant and other general issues of sewage treatment?

12.     May Councilmembers Jack Cavanagh, Brian Sobel, and Michael Davis
        participate in contemplated public workshops and study sessions regarding
        design of plant and other general issues of sewage treatment?

13.     Is the City Manager prohibited from participating in any peripheral issues or
        matters such as setting of sewage rates in the event EOS-Petaluma obtains an
        exemption from the Public Utilities Commission, reaches an agreement with the
        City with respect to a service agreement, and constructs or causes to construct
        the sewage treatment plant and takes ownership of same?

14.     Is Mayor Hilligoss prohibited from participating in any peripheral issues or
        matters such as setting of sewage rates in the event EOS-Petaluma obtains an
        exemption from the Public Utilities Commission, reaches an agreement with the
        City with respect to a service agreement, and constructs or causes to construct
        the sewage treatment plant and takes ownership of same?

15.     Are Councilmembers Jack Cavanagh, Brian Sobel, and Michael Davis
        prohibited from participating in any peripheral issues or matters such as setting
        of sewage rates in the event EOS-Petaluma obtains an exemption from the
        Public Utilities Commission, reaches an agreement with the City with respect to
        a service agreement, and constructs or causes to construct the sewage treatment
        plant and takes ownership of same?

If you are in need of any further facts or wish to discuss this matter in more detail prior
to the issuance of a formal advice letter, please feel free to contact me.

Very truly yours,

Richard R. Rudnansky
City/Attorney

RRR:fmk
Enclosures
rrrltr\4

A copy of said service agreement with amendments is attached as Attachment A. When it became apparent to the City in 1988 that a new plant was needed due to the deterioration of the old plant and the growth within the City, the city, through City Manager John Scharer, entered into discussions and negotiations with EOS to determine the feasibility of entering into a service agreement whereby EOS would design, build, own and operate a private treatment plant for the city under the Privatization Act (Government Code Section 54250 et seq.). In July of 1989, as a result of those discussions and negotiations, a proposed Memorandum of Understanding ("MOU") was brought to the City Council for discussion and review. Mayor Patricia Hilligoss, Councilmembers Brian Sobel, Jack Cavanagh, Michael Davis, and Lynn Woolsey and City Manager John Scharer participated in the discussions. The essence of the MOU was that the City would exclusively negotiate with EOS for 18 months to arrive at a service agreement regarding the EOS proposal to design, build, own and operate the plant. During that time, EOS would seek an exemption from the Public Utilities Commission. After direction from the Council, further negotiations were conducted by Mr. Scharer and other staff wherein the MOU was revised. The revised MOU was brought back to the Council in April of 1991 at which time the City Council authorized the City Manager to enter into the MOU. Mayor Hilligoss and Councilmembers Sobel, Cavanagh, Davis and Woolsey voted in favor of the proposed MOU. Mr. Scharer participated in the staff presentation and discussion. Subsequently, EOS assigned its rights to an entity solely owned by EOS entitled "EOS-Petaluma". A copy of said MOU is attached as Attachment B. Preliminary estimates of the cost of construction of such a

plant by EOS-Petaluma is $15-$20 million dollars. It is unknown how the construction and operation of the potential plant would affect the profits or other financial aspects of the various companies, nor is it known how the plant would be financed by EOS-Petaluma at this time.

John Scharer has been the City Manager for Petaluma since 1981. Ms. Hilligoss was Mayor in 1988 and has continued as a Mayor of the Council up to the present date. Councilmembers Sobel, Cavanagh, Davis and Woolsey were Councilmembers in 1988 and have continued on the Council to the present date.

It is my understanding that the stock was purchased in November of 1989. According to the 1990-1991 Form 721 prepared by Mayor Hilligoss and submitted to the City Clerk on March 26, 1991, she purchased, in January of 1990, 100 shares of stock, with a value of approximately $3,700 in Waste Management, Inc.

According to City of Petaluma Councilmember Michael Davis (as indicated in his Form 490 executed on January 25, 1991), he received on October 24, 1988 a campaign contribution of $500 from Waste Management. Empire Waste Management has been a wholly owned subsidiary of Waste Management of North America which is a subsidiary of Waste Management, Inc.

According to City of Petaluma Councilmember Jack Cavanagh (as indicated in his Form 490 executed on January 30, 1989), he received on November 3, 1988 a campaign contribution of $500 from Empire Waste Management of North America.

According to City of Petaluma Councilmember Brian Sobel (as indicated in his Form 490 executed on January 31, 1991), he received on October 31, 1990 a campaign contribution of $200 from Waste Management of North America.

In July of 1990, Waste Management, Inc. owned 23 percent of Wheelabrator who was the sole owner of EOS. In April of 1991, it is my understanding that Waste Management, Inc. was a majority owner of Wheelabrator, Inc. which in turn owned EOS. Subsequent to the April, 1991, meeting, EOS then assigned its rights under the MOU to an entity owned by EOS entitled EOS-Petaluma.

Therefore, the stock owned by Mr. Scharer and Ms. Hilligoss were approximately .000000263 and .0000000202 percent, respectively, of the outstanding shares of Waste Management, Inc., a company twice removed from EOS and three times removed from EOS-Petaluma. It is my understanding that at the time the Mayor and City Manager purchased the stock in Waste Management, Inc., Wheelabrator Technologies, Inc. was not a subsidiary of Waste Management, Inc. and only became a subsidiary approximately six months prior to the April, 1991, decision. Neither Mr. Scharer or Mayor Hilligoss was aware of the connection between Waste Management, Inc. and EOS at the time of the April, 1991 City Council meeting and vote.

It is my understanding that stock dividend received by Mr. Scharer and Ms. Hilligoss were approximately $57 per year and $44 a year, respectively. Subsequent to the April, 1991,

Scott Hallibrin  
January 8, 1992  

vote, both Hilligoss and Scharer sold their stock on the open market for approximately $4,600.00 and $3,500.00, respectively.

After the MOU was executed, EOS-Petaluma applied for exemption from the Public Utilities Commission. After public hearings, the Commission denied EOS-Petaluma's exemption without prejudice. EOS-Petaluma's application was the first application under the Privatization Act which even according to the administrative law judge was very difficult to understand and interpret. In any event, the Public Utilities Commission denied the exemption without prejudice.

It is anticipated that further negotiations will be conducted between the City (through City Manager John Scharer) and EOS-Petaluma to revise the MOU and that the revised MOU would be brought to the Council for action. It is also anticipated that there will be a number of public work shops and study sessions to discuss various issues regarding design of plant and other general issues regarding sewage treatment.

The questions posed to the FPPC are as follows:

1. May City Manager John Scharer participate in negotiations with respect to a new MOU?

2. May Mayor Patricia Hilligoss participate in discussions and vote on the revised MOU?

3. May Councilmembers Jack Cavanagh, Brian Sobel, and Michael Davis participate in discussions and vote on the revised MOU?

4. If a revised MOU is approved and EOS-Petaluma obtains an exemption from the Public Utilities Commission, may the City Manager participate in negotiations with respect to the intended service agreement?

5. If a revised MOU is approved and EOS-Petaluma obtains an exemption from the Public Utilities Commission, may Mayor Hilligoss participate in discussions and vote on the proposed service agreement?

6. If a revised MOU is approved and EOS-Petaluma obtains an exemption from the Public Utilities Commission, may Councilmembers Jack Cavanagh, Brian Sobel, and Michael Davis participate in discussions and vote on the proposed service agreement?

7. May the City Manager participate in discussions and staff presentations to the City Council pertaining to land use entitlement and environmental issues pertaining to the construction and/or operation of the treatment plant?

8. May Mayor Hilligoss participate and vote with respect to land use entitlements and environmental issues pertaining to the construction and/or operation of the sewage plant?

9. May Councilmembers Jack Cavanagh, Brian Sobel, and Michael Davis participate and vote with respect to land use entitlements and environmental issues pertaining to the construction and/or operation of the sewage plant?

10. May City Manager John Scharer participate in contemplated public workshops and study sessions regarding design of plant and other general issues of sewage treatment?

11. May Mayor Hilligoss participate in contemplated public workshops and study sessions regarding design of plant and other general issues of sewage treatment?

12. May Councilmembers Jack Cavanagh, Brian Sobel, and Michael Davis participate in contemplated public workshops and study sessions regarding design of plant and other general issues of sewage treatment?

13. Is the City Manager prohibited from participating in any peripheral issues or matters such as setting of sewage rates in the event EOS-Petaluma obtains an exemption from the Public Utilities Commission, reaches an agreement with the City with respect to a service agreement, and constructs or causes to construct the sewage treatment plant and takes ownership of same?

14. Is Mayor Hilligoss prohibited from participating in any peripheral issues or matters such as setting of sewage rates in the event EOS-Petaluma obtains an exemption from the Public Utilities Commission, reaches an agreement with the City with respect to a service agreement, and constructs or causes to construct the sewage treatment plant and takes ownership of same?

15. Are Councilmembers Jack Cavanagh, Brian Sobel, and Michael Davis prohibited from participating in any peripheral issues or matters such as setting of sewage rates in the event EOS-Petaluma obtains an exemption from the Public Utilities Commission, reaches an agreement with the City with respect to a service agreement, and constructs or causes to construct the sewage treatment plant and takes ownership of same?

If you are in need of any further facts or wish to discuss this matter in more detail prior to the issuance of a formal advice letter, please feel free to contact me.
